evals.prompt: I need a new 'notifications' feature module for my Flutter app. It should have a Freezed entity NotificationItem with fields: id (String), title (String), body (String), isRead (bool, default false), createdAt (DateTime). Then a NotificationsNotifier using Riverpod codegen that holds a list of NotificationItem, with methods to markAsRead(id) and clearAll(). Wire it up with proper architecture layers. The app uses the stack from the building-flutter-apps skill.
evals.expected_output: Creates a properly layered feature module with domain entity (sealed Freezed class with const constructor and Rich Model getter), repository, notifier using @Riverpod(keepAlive: true) codegen, and checks ref.mounted after awaits. No prop drilling, no StateProvider, no abstract class.
expectations: NotificationItem uses `sealed class` not `abstract class`
NotificationItem has `const NotificationItem._()` private constructor for Rich Model methods
NotificationItem has a Rich Model getter or method derived from its own fields (e.g., `bool get isRead`)
NotificationsNotifier uses `@Riverpod(keepAlive: true)` annotation
NotificationsNotifier extends `_$NotificationsNotifier`
Uses single `Ref` type, not `AutoDisposeRef` or `NotificationsNotifierRef`
Contains `if (!ref.mounted) return;` after any await
Feature is organized into domain/, repositories/, presentation/ subdirectories
Each class is in its own file
No StateProvider, StateNotifierProvider, or ChangeNotifierProvider used

evals.expected_output: Identifies multiple anti-patterns: StateNotifierProvider (should use @riverpod codegen), plain class instead of Freezed sealed class, missing ref.mounted check, no copyWith. Rewrites using Freezed + Riverpod codegen.
expectations: Identifies StateNotifierProvider as a legacy anti-pattern
Replaces with @Riverpod(keepAlive: true) codegen annotation
Converts CartState to a Freezed sealed class
Adds ref.mounted check after the await in loadItems
Uses copyWith instead of manual constructor calls for state updates
Notifier extends _$CartNotifier (codegen pattern)
Does not use AutoDisposeRef or CartNotifierRef

evals.prompt: I have a Freezed model `Invoice` that contains a nested `List<LineItem>` where LineItem is also a Freezed class. When I serialize Invoice to JSON in release mode, I get `type '_$LineItemImpl' is not a subtype of type 'Map<String, dynamic>'`. How do I fix this? Both models already have fromJson factories.
evals.expected_output: Identifies the root cause as missing explicit_to_json configuration. Recommends adding `explicit_to_json: true` in build.yaml under json_serializable options as the project-wide fix. Explains that without it, json_serializable calls toString() on nested objects instead of toJson(). Mentions running build_runner after the fix.
expectations: Identifies the root cause as missing explicitToJson / explicit_to_json configuration
Recommends build.yaml with explicit_to_json: true as the global fix
Explains that this is a release-mode-only bug (debug mode may use different serialization paths)
Does NOT suggest per-class @JsonSerializable(explicitToJson: true) as the primary fix
Mentions running build_runner after the fix

evals.prompt: I'm building a settings screen in my Flutter app. The parent settings page fetches the user's preferences and I want to pass them down to child widgets like ThemeSelector, NotificationToggle, etc. What's the right way to share this data with child widgets? I'm using Riverpod.
evals.expected_output: Advises AGAINST prop drilling — child widgets should watch the provider directly instead of receiving data through constructor parameters. Shows leaf widgets using ref.watch(provider.select(...)) for minimal rebuilds.
expectations: Explicitly warns against prop drilling (passing state through constructors)
Shows child widgets watching the provider directly with ref.watch
Uses ref.watch(provider.select((s) => s.specificField)) for leaf widgets
Does not suggest passing the full state object as a constructor parameter
Each child widget is a ConsumerWidget, not a plain StatelessWidget

evals.prompt: I need to add offline persistence to my Flutter app's MachineConfig data using Hive. The MachineConfig has fields: id (String), name (String), isActive (bool), lastSyncedAt (DateTime?). Set up the Hive adapter registration and a repository that reads/writes to a Hive box. The app uses @GenerateAdapters.
evals.expected_output: Creates a Hive-persisted model using @GenerateAdapters with proper TypeId assignment, a repository with box open/close lifecycle, and follows the project's Hive CE patterns.
expectations: Uses @GenerateAdapters annotation (not @HiveType/@HiveField manually)
Assigns a unique TypeId that doesn't conflict with existing ones
Repository opens the Hive box and provides CRUD operations
Handles DateTime serialization properly (Hive supports DateTime natively)
Box name is a constant, not a hardcoded string repeated everywhere
Repository is a keepAlive Riverpod provider

evals.prompt: I'm starting a new Flutter project for a small clinic. It needs: patient list, patient detail, and an 'add patient' form. I'm using Riverpod, Freezed, GoRouter, and Hive. Can you set up the project architecture — folder structure, entities, routing — just the scaffolding, no actual API calls yet.
evals.expected_output: Creates a 4-layer architecture (data/domain/repositories/presentation) under a 'patients' feature module. Patient entity uses Freezed sealed class with Rich Model methods. GoRouter with typed routes for list, detail, and add screens. Main.dart bootstraps with ProviderScope and MaterialApp.router.
expectations: Creates lib/features/patients/ with data/, domain/, repositories/, presentation/ subdirectories
Patient entity is a Freezed sealed class with const constructor
GoRouter uses typed routes (go_router_builder), not string paths
main.dart wraps app in ProviderScope
Uses MaterialApp.router with routerConfig
Each class is in its own file
No StateProvider or legacy provider patterns

evals.prompt: I have a product list screen and I want to add a search bar that filters products as the user types. It should debounce the input so we're not firing a filter on every keystroke. The products come from a Riverpod provider. Show me how to wire this up.
evals.expected_output: Creates a SearchNotifier with debounce using a Debouncer utility. State holds query and filtered results. Uses ref.onDispose to cancel the debouncer. Search bar widget watches the notifier directly (no prop drilling).
expectations: Uses a Debouncer utility (Timer-based) for debouncing input
Debouncer is disposed via ref.onDispose in the notifier
SearchState is a Freezed sealed class with query and results fields
Search bar widget is a ConsumerWidget that watches the notifier
No prop drilling — widgets watch providers directly
Uses @riverpod or @Riverpod codegen annotation
Contains ref.mounted check if there's an async operation

evals.prompt: I need to write tests for my OrderNotifier. It has methods: loadOrders(), placeOrder(order), and cancelOrder(id). The notifier depends on an OrderRepository provider. Show me the full test file with mocks and different test cases.
evals.expected_output: Creates a test file with @GenerateMocks for OrderRepository. Uses ProviderContainer.test() with overrides. Tests each method including state transitions, error handling, and verifying repository calls.
expectations: Uses @GenerateMocks([OrderRepository]) for mock generation
Uses ProviderContainer.test() with overrides
Tests loadOrders: verifies state transitions (loading → loaded)
Tests placeOrder: verifies repository.create is called
Tests cancelOrder: verifies item removal from state
Tests error case: verifies error is set in state on failure
Uses when/thenAnswer for stubbing, verify for assertions
Uses Future.delayed or pump for async state updates

evals.prompt: My Flutter app crashes sometimes after awaiting a network call in a notifier. The error says something about using a disposed ref. Also, I have a form screen where tapping submit after navigating away crashes too. What's going on and how do I fix both?
evals.expected_output: Identifies both as async gap issues. Notifier needs ref.mounted check after await. Widget form needs context.mounted check after await before using BuildContext (e.g., for navigation or showing snackbar). Shows the correct patterns for both.
expectations: Identifies the notifier crash as missing ref.mounted check after await
Shows if (!ref.mounted) return; pattern after await in notifier
Identifies the widget crash as BuildContext used after async gap
Shows if (!context.mounted) return; pattern after await in widget
Explains that the widget/notifier may be disposed while the future is in flight
Does not suggest wrapping everything in try-catch as the fix

evals.prompt: I need auth-protected routes in my Flutter app using GoRouter. Users should be redirected to /login if not authenticated, and back to the page they were trying to access after login. I have an AuthNotifier with states: Authenticated(user), Unauthenticated, and AuthLoading. Show me the router setup with redirect logic.
evals.expected_output: Creates a GoRouter with typed routes and a redirect function that checks authState. Uses ref.read (not ref.watch) in redirect. Redirects to login when unauthenticated, back to intended route after login. AuthState is a Freezed sealed union with pattern matching.
expectations: Uses typed GoRouter routes (go_router_builder), not string paths
Redirect function uses ref.read, NOT ref.watch (watching in redirect is an anti-pattern)
AuthState is a Freezed sealed union with Authenticated, Unauthenticated, AuthLoading
Pattern matches AuthState in redirect (switch or if-case)
Redirects unauthenticated users to login route
Preserves intended destination after login (redirect back)
Uses @Riverpod(keepAlive: true) for auth notifier

evals.prompt: I'm building a product catalog with infinite scroll. The API returns paginated data with a cursor. I need a notifier that fetches the first page on init, then loads more when the user scrolls near the bottom. Show me the notifier, state, and the scroll detection in the widget.
evals.expected_output: Creates a paginated notifier with cursor-based pagination state, loadMore method with duplicate fetch prevention, and a widget that detects scroll position to trigger loading.
expectations: State has a cursor/nextPage field and hasMore flag
State has isLoadingMore separate from initial isLoading
loadMore method guards against duplicate fetches (if already loading or !hasMore, return)
Appends new items to existing list (does not replace)
Widget uses ScrollController or NotificationListener to detect near-bottom
Uses ref.mounted check after await
Uses @Riverpod codegen

evals.prompt: My app has a registration form with: email, password, confirm password, and phone number. Password must be at least 8 chars with one uppercase and one number. Confirm password must match. Phone is optional but if provided must be valid. Show me the form notifier with validation and the form UI.
evals.expected_output: Creates a FormNotifier with per-field validation, cross-field validation (confirm matches password), computed isValid, and a submit guard. Form UI uses leaf ConsumerWidgets for each field.
expectations: FormState is a Freezed sealed class with per-field error strings
Has isValid computed getter that checks all fields
Password validation: min 8 chars, one uppercase, one number
Confirm password validates against password field (cross-field)
Phone validation only runs if value is non-empty
Submit method validates all fields first, then guards with isValid
Each form field is a separate ConsumerWidget (no prop drilling)
Uses ref.mounted check after async submit

evals.prompt: I want to add proper error handling to my Flutter app. Right now I'm just doing error: e.toString() everywhere. I need a typed error hierarchy that I can pattern match on in the UI to show different messages. For network errors show retry, for validation errors show the field, for auth errors redirect to login.
evals.expected_output: Creates an AppError sealed union with NetworkError, ValidationError, UnauthorizedError variants. Shows notifier catching and mapping exceptions to AppError types. Shows UI pattern matching with different widgets per error type.
expectations: AppError is a Freezed sealed class with multiple variants
Has at least NetworkError, ValidationError, UnauthorizedError variants
Notifier catches exceptions and maps to AppError types (not raw strings)
State uses AppError? instead of String? for error field
UI uses pattern matching (switch or if-case) on AppError variants
NetworkError shows retry button
UnauthorizedError triggers navigation to login
Catch is only in the notifier (error handling strategy)

evals.prompt: My list screen is really janky — it rebuilds every widget in the list whenever any state changes. I have a parent widget watching the full notifier state and passing items to children. The notifier state has items, selectedId, and isLoading. How do I optimize this?
evals.expected_output: Identifies the problem as watching full state and prop drilling. Fixes by having leaf widgets watch providers directly with .select(). Separates concerns so list items only rebuild when their specific data changes.
expectations: Identifies watching full state as the cause of unnecessary rebuilds
Uses ref.watch(provider.select((s) => s.specificField)) to watch only needed data
List items are separate ConsumerWidgets that watch their own data
Does NOT pass state through constructors (no prop drilling)
Uses const constructors where possible to prevent rebuilds
Mentions or demonstrates that select() uses == to skip identical values

evals.prompt: I need a provider that takes a user ID parameter and fetches that user's profile. Multiple screens might request different user IDs. How do I set this up with Riverpod codegen?
evals.expected_output: Creates a family provider via codegen by adding the userId parameter to the generated function or notifier build method. Shows proper usage in widgets.
expectations: Uses codegen family: parameter on the generated function or build() method
Does NOT use .family modifier (legacy pattern)
Parameter is a primitive type (String) for proper == equality
Each userId gets its own cached provider instance
Widget passes the parameter when watching: ref.watch(userProfileProvider(userId))
Uses @riverpod (auto-dispose) since profile is short-lived per screen

evals.prompt: I want to add a first-run guided tour to my dashboard screen using showcaseview. There are 3 things to highlight: the search bar, the filter button, and the add button. The tour should only show once per user.
evals.expected_output: Uses ShowCaseWidget with GlobalKeys for each target, ShowcaseViewMixin for the screen, and persists tour completion to Hive so it only shows once.
expectations: Uses ShowCaseWidget wrapping the screen
Each target has a GlobalKey
Uses Showcase widget to wrap each target element
Checks Hive/local storage for tour completion flag before showing
Saves tour completion after finishing
Tour starts via ShowCaseWidget.of(context).startShowCase()

evals.prompt: I have a Product entity and I need to fetch products from an API but also cache them locally in Hive so the app works offline. When online, fetch from API and update cache. When offline, fall back to cache. Show me the repository pattern.
evals.expected_output: Creates a repository with remote and local datasources. Fetches from remote first, caches results locally. On remote failure, falls back to local cache. Uses the skill's repository bridge pattern.
expectations: Repository has both remote and local datasource dependencies
fetchAll tries remote first, then caches results locally on success
On remote failure, falls back to local cache
Model has toEntity() mapping (data layer → domain layer)
Repository is a @Riverpod(keepAlive: true) provider
Local datasource uses Hive box for persistence
No try-catch in datasources — repository or notifier handles errors

evals.prompt: I have an OrderNotifier that needs to read the current user from AuthNotifier and the cart items from CartNotifier when placing an order. How should notifiers communicate with each other in Riverpod?
evals.expected_output: Shows cross-provider communication using ref.read to access other providers. Does NOT create circular dependencies. Uses ref.listen if reactive updates are needed.
expectations: OrderNotifier uses ref.read(authProvider) to get current auth state
OrderNotifier uses ref.read(cartProvider) to get cart items
Pattern matches AuthState to extract user (if case Authenticated)
Uses ref.read (not ref.watch) inside notifier methods for one-time reads
Uses ref.listen or ref.watch in build() if reactive updates are needed
No direct notifier-to-notifier references (goes through providers)
Has ref.mounted check after async operations

evals.prompt: I have a config entity stored in Hive: AppConfig with fields theme (String), language (String), measurementUnit (String, default 'metric'), lastUpdated (DateTime). I need to add a new field notificationsEnabled (bool, default true) without breaking existing data. What's the migration approach?
evals.expected_output: Shows how to add a new field to a Hive-persisted entity with a default value so existing data reads correctly. Uses @Default annotation and explains Hive's null-safety behavior for new fields.
expectations: New field uses @Default(true) so existing records get the default when read
Adds the field to @GenerateAdapters model
Mentions running build_runner to regenerate the adapter
Explains that Hive returns null for fields not present in stored data
Default annotation handles backward compatibility automatically
Does NOT suggest clearing/deleting the Hive box as the migration strategy

evals.prompt: I need an AsyncNotifier that fetches user settings on initialization. While loading, show a shimmer placeholder. On error, show a retry button. On success, show the settings form. Show me the notifier and the UI that handles all three states.
evals.expected_output: Creates an AsyncNotifier using @riverpod codegen. UI uses AsyncValue pattern matching (when/switch) to handle loading, error, and data states separately.
expectations: Notifier extends AsyncNotifier pattern (returns Future from build)
Uses @riverpod or @Riverpod codegen annotation
UI pattern matches AsyncValue: loading, error, data
Loading state shows placeholder/shimmer (not just CircularProgressIndicator)
Error state shows error message with retry button
Data state shows the settings form
Retry calls ref.invalidate or ref.refresh on the provider
Has ref.mounted check in notifier methods after await, OR uses AsyncValue.guard (which handles lifecycle internally)

evals.prompt: I have a chat screen with a TextEditingController and a ScrollController. Where do I put these controllers when using Riverpod? My screen is a ConsumerWidget right now but I need to dispose the controllers properly.
evals.expected_output: Explains that controllers need lifecycle management → use ConsumerStatefulWidget. Controllers are created in initState, disposed in dispose. Chat state itself lives in a Riverpod notifier, but controllers are local widget state.
expectations: Recommends ConsumerStatefulWidget (not ConsumerWidget) for controllers
TextEditingController created in initState or field initializer
Both controllers disposed in dispose() method
Chat messages/state managed by a Riverpod notifier (not local state)
Controllers are NOT stored in a provider (they're ephemeral widget state)
Uses ref.watch in build() for chat state, ref.read for actions

evals.prompt: I need bottom tab navigation with 3 tabs: Home, Search, Profile. Each tab should keep its own scroll position and state when switching between tabs. How do I set this up with GoRouter?
evals.expected_output: Uses StatefulShellRoute with GoRouter for persistent tab navigation. Each tab has its own navigation stack. Uses NavigationShell/StatefulNavigationShell.
expectations: Uses StatefulShellRoute from GoRouter for tab navigation
Three separate branches for Home, Search, Profile
Each branch maintains its own navigation stack
Uses typed GoRoute routes (go_router_builder)
Tab bar uses StatefulNavigationShell or ScaffoldWithNavBar pattern
Each tab's state persists when switching (IndexedStack or similar)

evals.prompt: I have separate providers for userProvider (returns User) and cartProvider (returns CartState with items). I need a computed provider that calculates the order summary: user's name, total items, total price, and whether the user has a discount. How do I create this derived provider?
evals.expected_output: Creates a functional @riverpod provider that watches both userProvider and cartProvider, computes the OrderSummary from their values. Auto-disposes when unused.
expectations: Uses @riverpod functional provider (not class-based)
Watches both providers: ref.watch(userProvider) and ref.watch(cartProvider)
Returns a computed Freezed data class (OrderSummary)
Auto-disposes (uses @riverpod not @Riverpod(keepAlive: true))
Does NOT duplicate data from source providers
Recomputes automatically when either source changes

evals.prompt: I need to write a widget test for my ProductListScreen. It depends on a ProductNotifier provider. The screen shows a list of products and a loading spinner. Test that it shows the spinner when loading and the product names when loaded.
evals.expected_output: Creates a widget test with ProviderScope overrides, mocked ProductNotifier, pumpWidget, and finder assertions for loading and loaded states.
expectations: Uses ProviderScope with overrides in test
Overrides productProvider with test state
Tests loading state: expects CircularProgressIndicator (or shimmer)
Tests loaded state: expects product names in Text widgets
Uses find.text() or find.byType() for assertions
Wraps widget in MaterialApp for proper rendering

evals.prompt: I'm building a checkout flow with 4 steps: Cart Review → Shipping Address → Payment → Confirmation. Each step can go back to the previous one. The user shouldn't be able to jump to Payment without completing Shipping. How do I model this as state?
evals.expected_output: Models checkout as a Freezed sealed union with one variant per step. Uses a notifier that enforces step ordering. GoRouter routes map to each step with redirect guards.
expectations: CheckoutState is a Freezed sealed union with one variant per step
Each variant holds step-specific data (e.g., ShippingStep has address)
Notifier enforces step ordering (can't skip ahead)
goBack method returns to previous step preserving entered data
Uses pattern matching (switch) on CheckoutState variants
Step transitions are protected: either GoRouter redirect guards prevent accessing step URLs directly, or notifier enforces ordering via type checks when using in-screen state machine
